title: Adenocarcinoma (Lung)
summary: Report as 'adenocarcinoma pattern suspected; recommend permanent sections and immunohistochemistry for confirmation.'
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 65‑year‑old smoker with a 2 cm peripheral lung nodule.
- IOC Request: Is this nodule malignant and, if so, what type?
- Description: Frozen section shows gland‑forming tumor with lepidic growth along alveolar septae. Tumor cells have enlarged nuclei, prominent nucleoli and produce intracellular mucin. Alveolar spaces may be filled with mucin pools.

## Sketch
- Lepidic pattern lining alveolar walls
- Glandular formations with mucin production
- Absence of keratin pearls or intercellular bridges

## Pitfall
Collapse of alveolar spaces on frozen can simulate invasion; mucin lakes can be misinterpreted as necrosis.

## Consequence
Overcalling invasion can prompt unnecessary lobectomy; undercalling carcinoma may leave residual disease requiring repeat surgery.

## Action
Report as 'adenocarcinoma pattern suspected; recommend permanent sections and immunohistochemistry for confirmation.'

title: Cholangiocarcinoma
summary: Report presence or absence of infiltrating glands; when uncertain, describe atypia and defer to permanent sections and immunostains.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 65‑year‑old with jaundice and hilar bile duct stricture.
- IOC Request: Is this bile duct lesion malignant or benign?
- Description: Frozen section shows gland‑forming tumor with desmoplastic stroma infiltrating the bile duct wall. The glands are irregular and lined by cuboidal to columnar cells with nuclear atypia and mitoses. Mucin production is common.

## Sketch
- Irregular infiltrating glands in fibrous stroma
- Desmoplastic response around glands
- Mucin production within lumina

## Pitfall
Benign peribiliary glands and reactive atypia can mimic carcinoma; sclerosing cholangitis may produce fibrosis without invasion.

## Consequence
False positive diagnosis may result in unnecessary extensive resection; false negative may miss a curable cancer.

## Action
Report presence or absence of infiltrating glands; when uncertain, describe atypia and defer to permanent sections and immunostains.

title: Chromophobe Renal Cell Carcinoma
summary: Describe features and suggest chromophobe RCC when classic; recommend correlation with permanents and immunohistochemistry (CK7, CD117).
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 64‑year‑old with 5 cm renal mass suspicious for oncocytoma or chromophobe carcinoma.
- IOC Request: Is this chromophobe carcinoma, oncocytoma, or other lesion?
- Description: Frozen section shows large polygonal cells with pale eosinophilic cytoplasm and prominent cell borders. Perinuclear clearing (halo) and wrinkled ('raisinoid') nuclei are characteristic.

## Sketch
- Large cells with well‑defined borders
- Perinuclear halos
- Wrinkled raisinoid nuclei

## Pitfall
Oncocytoma can show similar features but lacks perinuclear halos; frozen artifact may exaggerate cytoplasmic clearing.

## Consequence
Misclassification can alter surveillance strategy as chromophobe RCC has different behavior than oncocytoma.

## Action
Describe features and suggest chromophobe RCC when classic; recommend correlation with permanents and immunohistochemistry (CK7, CD117).

title: Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma
summary: Report presence of clear cell carcinoma at margin if identified; when uncertain, describe features and defer to permanent sections and immunostains.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 55‑year‑old with 4 cm enhancing renal cortical mass.
- IOC Request: Is the margin free of clear cell carcinoma and is this lesion malignant?
- Description: Frozen section shows nests and sheets of cells with abundant clear cytoplasm and distinct cell borders separated by a delicate vascular network (chicken‑wire vasculature). Nuclei vary from small round (low grade) to pleomorphic with macronucleoli (high grade).

## Sketch
- Sheets of clear cells with distinct borders
- Delicate branching vasculature
- Variation in nuclear grade

## Pitfall
Oncocytoma and chromophobe RCC may show clear cells focally; frozen artifact can cause clearing of cytoplasm in other tumors.

## Consequence
Misclassification affects extent of resection and need for further surgery.

## Action
Report presence of clear cell carcinoma at margin if identified; when uncertain, describe features and defer to permanent sections and immunostains.

title: Cribriform Carcinoma (Breast)
summary: Comment on cribriform architecture and presence/absence of myoepithelial cells; recommend permanent sections and immunohistochemistry to confirm invasion.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 60‑year‑old with palpable breast mass and microcalcifications.
- IOC Request: Is this lesion invasive cribriform carcinoma or in situ disease?
- Description: Frozen section shows nests of tumor cells with well‑defined borders forming sieve‑like (cribriform) spaces. The lumina are round and punched‑out, giving a 'Swiss cheese' appearance. Invasive carcinoma infiltrates stroma without a myoepithelial layer.

## Sketch
- Nested tumor with cribriform pattern
- Uniform round punched‑out spaces
- Absence of myoepithelial cells in invasive foci

## Pitfall
Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) with cribriform pattern retains a myoepithelial layer and may be confined within ducts; differentiating on frozen can be challenging.

## Consequence
Underdiagnosis of invasion can lead to inadequate margins; overdiagnosis may lead to overtreatment.

## Action
Comment on cribriform architecture and presence/absence of myoepithelial cells; recommend permanent sections and immunohistochemistry to confirm invasion.

title: Desmoplastic Stroma Modal (Pancreas)
summary: If no glands are identified, report as 'fibrotic/desmoplastic stroma, no definitive carcinoma identified'; recommend thorough sampling and permanent sections.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 70‑year‑old with obstructive jaundice; intraoperative frozen of pancreatic margin shows dense fibrosis.
- IOC Request: Does this desmoplastic stroma represent invasive carcinoma or chronic pancreatitis?
- Description: Frozen section reveals dense, hyalinized stroma with scattered spindled fibroblasts. No obvious glandular structures are seen. Chronic pancreatitis shows lobular atrophy and interlobular fibrosis with residual small ducts.

## Sketch
- Dense fibrous stroma without glands
- Residual acinar tissue in chronic pancreatitis
- Compare with invasive glands in carcinoma

## Pitfall
Frozen artifact may obscure small infiltrating glands; desmoplastic stroma alone is not diagnostic of carcinoma.

## Consequence
Misinterpreting fibrosis as carcinoma leads to unnecessary extension of resection; missing small invasive glands leaves tumor behind.

## Action
If no glands are identified, report as 'fibrotic/desmoplastic stroma, no definitive carcinoma identified'; recommend thorough sampling and permanent sections.

title: Ductal Lesion (Pancreas)
summary: Classify as low‑, intermediate‑ or high‑grade intraepithelial neoplasia when possible; avoid diagnosing invasion on frozen; recommend permanent evaluation.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 45‑year‑old with chronic pancreatitis and dilated pancreatic duct on imaging.
- IOC Request: Is this duct lesion benign (PanIN) or carcinoma in situ?
- Description: Frozen section shows dilated ducts lined by epithelial cells with varying degrees of atypia. Low‑grade PanIN exhibits tall columnar mucinous epithelium with minimal atypia, whereas high‑grade PanIN demonstrates cribriform architecture and marked nuclear atypia without stromal invasion.

## Sketch
- Dilated duct with tall columnar epithelium
- Cribriform epithelial proliferation in high‑grade lesions
- Absence of stromal invasion

## Pitfall
Frozen sections may exaggerate nuclear atypia; reactive duct changes in chronic pancreatitis can mimic PanIN.

## Consequence
Overcalling high‑grade dysplasia as invasive carcinoma may prompt unnecessary resection; undercalling can miss an opportunity for curative surgery.

## Action
Classify as low‑, intermediate‑ or high‑grade intraepithelial neoplasia when possible; avoid diagnosing invasion on frozen; recommend permanent evaluation.

title: Endometrioid Carcinoma (Uterus/Ovary)
summary: Assign grade based on architectural and nuclear features; recommend correlation with permanents for final grade and depth of myometrial invasion.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 60‑year‑old with abnormal uterine bleeding and endometrial mass.
- IOC Request: Is this lesion endometrioid carcinoma and what grade?
- Description: Frozen section shows back‑to‑back glands resembling endometrial glands with confluent architecture. Low‑grade tumors demonstrate well‑formed glands with minimal atypia; high‑grade tumors show solid growth and marked atypia.

## Sketch
- Back‑to‑back endometrioid glands
- Solid growth areas in high‑grade lesions
- Nuclear atypia corresponding to grade

## Pitfall
Hyperplastic endometrium with atypia may mimic low‑grade carcinoma; frozen sections may under‑grade tumors.

## Consequence
Undergrading may result in inadequate staging; overgrading can lead to overtreatment.

## Action
Assign grade based on architectural and nuclear features; recommend correlation with permanents for final grade and depth of myometrial invasion.

title: Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma (Glandular)
summary: If infiltrative glands are present, report 'invasive adenocarcinoma at margin' versus 'no invasive carcinoma identified'; when uncertain, defer to permanent sections.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 62‑year‑old with jaundice and pancreatic head mass undergoing Whipple procedure.
- IOC Request: Is the resection margin free of invasive carcinoma?
- Description: Frozen section of the pancreatic neck margin shows infiltrative, angulated glands embedded in desmoplastic stroma. The glands are haphazardly arranged, irregular in shape and size, and lined by atypical epithelial cells with enlarged nuclei and mitotic figures.

## Sketch
- Haphazard infiltrative glands at the margin
- Desmoplastic stroma encasing irregular ducts
- Atypical cells with enlarged nuclei

## Pitfall
Benign pancreatic ducts entrapped by inflammation can mimic carcinoma; freezing artifact may exaggerate nuclear atypia.

## Consequence
False positive margin involvement leads to unnecessary additional resection; false negative may leave residual tumor requiring adjuvant therapy.

## Action
If infiltrative glands are present, report 'invasive adenocarcinoma at margin' versus 'no invasive carcinoma identified'; when uncertain, defer to permanent sections.

title: Glioma
summary: Comment on features suggestive of low‑ versus high‑grade glioma; recommend correlation with MRI and permanent sections.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 30‑year‑old with seizures and frontal lobe mass.
- IOC Request: Is this lesion a low‑grade glioma or high‑grade glioblastoma?
- Description: Frozen section and crush prep show glial fibrillary background with scattered atypical astrocytic cells. Low‑grade gliomas exhibit mild atypia and low cellularity, whereas high‑grade gliomas show marked pleomorphism, microvascular proliferation and necrosis.

## Sketch
- Fibrillary glial background
- Microvascular proliferation in high‑grade tumors
- Pseudopalisading necrosis in glioblastoma

## Pitfall
Frozen section may underrepresent cellularity; demyelinating lesions can mimic low‑grade gliomas.

## Consequence
Accurate grading guides extent of resection and adjuvant therapy.

## Action
Comment on features suggestive of low‑ versus high‑grade glioma; recommend correlation with MRI and permanent sections.

title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma
summary: Describe features suggestive of hepatocellular carcinoma; recommend correlation with AFP, imaging and permanent sections.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 58‑year‑old with chronic hepatitis C and a 4 cm hepatic mass.
- IOC Request: Is this mass hepatocellular carcinoma versus regenerative nodule?
- Description: Frozen section shows trabecular pattern of hepatocyte‑like cells with increased nuclear‑to‑cytoplasmic ratio and thickened cell plates (>3 cells thick). Endothelial wrapping and absence of portal tracts are noted.

## Sketch
- Thickened trabecular cords
- Loss of portal tracts
- Endothelial cell wrapping around tumor plates

## Pitfall
Macroregenerative and dysplastic nodules may show mild trabecular thickening but retain portal tracts; freezing artifact can obscure sinusoidal pattern.

## Consequence
Underdiagnosing carcinoma delays definitive resection; overdiagnosing cirrhotic nodules as carcinoma may lead to unnecessary surgery.

## Action
Describe features suggestive of hepatocellular carcinoma; recommend correlation with AFP, imaging and permanent sections.

title: Lymphoma Modal (Head & Neck)
summary: If lymphoma is suspected, recommend flow cytometry and permanent sections; avoid definitive subtyping on frozen section alone.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 60‑year‑old with rapidly enlarging cervical lymph node and systemic B‑symptoms.
- IOC Request: Is this lymph node involved by lymphoma or metastatic carcinoma?
- Description: Frozen section shows diffuse effacement of nodal architecture by sheets of atypical lymphoid cells. Depending on subtype, cells may be small and monotonous or large with prominent nucleoli. Sinusoidal architecture is lost.

## Sketch
- Diffuse sheets of atypical lymphoid cells
- Loss of normal nodal architecture
- Variation in cell size depending on subtype

## Pitfall
Reactive hyperplasia can produce effacement and polymorphous infiltrates; freezing artifact can distort cell morphology.

## Consequence
Misclassifying lymphoma as carcinoma may lead to inappropriate surgery; misdiagnosing reactive changes as lymphoma results in overtreatment.

## Action
If lymphoma is suspected, recommend flow cytometry and permanent sections; avoid definitive subtyping on frozen section alone.

title: Meningioma
summary: Describe classical meningioma features and comment on cellularity and mitoses; recommend permanents for definitive grading.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 55‑year‑old with dural‑based mass and headaches.
- IOC Request: Is this lesion a meningioma and does it show atypical or malignant features?
- Description: Frozen section shows whorled bundles of spindle cells with psammoma bodies and a collagenous matrix. Cells have ovoid nuclei with fine chromatin. Atypical meningioma shows increased cellularity, mitoses and necrosis.

## Sketch
- Whorled architecture of spindle cells
- Psammoma bodies
- Assessment of cellularity and mitoses

## Pitfall
Hemangiopericytoma and metastatic carcinoma can mimic meningioma; frozen may not accurately reflect mitotic index.

## Consequence
Undergrading atypical meningioma may lead to recurrence; overcalling benign meningioma as atypical results in unnecessary aggressive treatment.

## Action
Describe classical meningioma features and comment on cellularity and mitoses; recommend permanents for definitive grading.

title: Metastatic Tumor (CNS)
summary: Report features suggestive of metastasis; recommend correlation with clinical history and immunohistochemistry.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 65‑year‑old with known lung carcinoma and new brain lesion.
- IOC Request: Is this brain lesion metastatic carcinoma or primary glial tumor?
- Description: Frozen section shows well‑circumscribed lesion composed of cohesive epithelial cells forming glands and sheets, sharply demarcated from adjacent brain parenchyma. Tumor lacks glial fibrillary background.

## Sketch
- Sharp demarcation between tumor and brain
- Cohesive epithelial nests or glands
- Absence of glial fibrillary background

## Pitfall
High‑grade gliomas can have sharp borders; meningiomas can mimic metastasis when invasive.

## Consequence
Distinguishing metastasis from primary glioma determines need for systemic workup and guides surgical extent.

## Action
Report features suggestive of metastasis; recommend correlation with clinical history and immunohistochemistry.

title: Metastatic Tumor (Liver)
summary: Describe features of metastatic adenocarcinoma; recommend immunohistochemistry on permanents to determine primary site.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 50‑year‑old with known colon cancer and new hepatic lesion.
- IOC Request: Is this hepatic lesion metastatic adenocarcinoma or primary hepatocellular carcinoma?
- Description: Frozen section shows glands with dirty necrosis infiltrating hepatic parenchyma. Tumor cells form cribriform and tubular structures distinct from surrounding hepatocytes. Background liver may be relatively uninvolved.

## Sketch
- Glandular structures with dirty necrosis
- Sharp demarcation between tumor and hepatocytes
- Absence of hepatocellular trabecular pattern

## Pitfall
Poorly differentiated metastatic tumors can mimic hepatocellular carcinoma; cholangiocarcinoma may also resemble metastasis.

## Consequence
Accurate classification determines need for hepatic resection versus systemic therapy.

## Action
Describe features of metastatic adenocarcinoma; recommend immunohistochemistry on permanents to determine primary site.

title: Mucinous Tumor (Ovary)
summary: Assess for invasion in multiple sections; when in doubt between borderline and invasive, defer to permanent sections and recommend complete staging.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 50‑year‑old with large unilateral ovarian cystic mass.
- IOC Request: Is this mucinous neoplasm benign, borderline, or invasive carcinoma?
- Description: Frozen section shows multiloculated cyst lined by mucin‑secreting columnar epithelium. Borderline mucinous tumors display epithelial stratification, tufting, and mild atypia without stromal invasion. Invasive carcinoma exhibits complex glands and destructive invasion into the stroma.

## Sketch
- Multiloculated cystic architecture
- Epithelial stratification and tufting in borderline tumors
- Stromal invasion in carcinoma

## Pitfall
Sampling error on frozen may miss foci of invasion; pseudomyxoma peritonei may originate from appendiceal tumors.

## Consequence
Incorrect classification affects staging and extent of surgery.

## Action
Assess for invasion in multiple sections; when in doubt between borderline and invasive, defer to permanent sections and recommend complete staging.

title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma
summary: If features are definitive, report 'papillary thyroid carcinoma'; otherwise describe nuclear atypia and defer to permanent sections.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 32‑year‑old woman with a 1.5 cm thyroid nodule and cervical lymphadenopathy.
- IOC Request: Is this papillary thyroid carcinoma and are margins free of tumor?
- Description: Frozen section shows papillary fronds lined by cells with enlarged overlapping nuclei, nuclear grooves, and intranuclear pseudoinclusions. Psammoma bodies are present.

## Sketch
- True papillary architecture with fibrovascular cores
- Nuclear grooves and pseudoinclusions
- Psammoma bodies in the stroma

## Pitfall
Hashimoto thyroiditis and reactive changes can show nuclear grooves; freezing artifact can create pseudoinclusions.

## Consequence
False positive diagnosis may lead to unnecessary total thyroidectomy; false negative may delay definitive treatment.

## Action
If features are definitive, report 'papillary thyroid carcinoma'; otherwise describe nuclear atypia and defer to permanent sections.

title: Papillary Renal Cell Carcinoma
summary: Report papillary carcinoma when typical features present; when uncertain, describe architecture and recommend permanent sections and immunohistochemistry.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 70‑year‑old with incidental 3 cm renal mass on CT.
- IOC Request: Is this papillary renal cell carcinoma and are the margins free of tumor?
- Description: Frozen section shows papillary and tubulopapillary structures lined by cells with eosinophilic to basophilic cytoplasm. Foamy macrophages may fill papillary cores. Psammoma bodies can be present.

## Sketch
- Papillary and tubulopapillary architecture
- Foamy macrophages in papillary cores
- Possible psammoma bodies

## Pitfall
Necrotic areas in clear cell RCC can mimic papillary structures; metanephric adenoma may show papillary patterns.

## Consequence
Accurate subtyping influences prognosis and follow‑up.

## Action
Report papillary carcinoma when typical features present; when uncertain, describe architecture and recommend permanent sections and immunohistochemistry.

title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma Metastasis (Head & Neck)
summary: If features are classic, report metastatic papillary thyroid carcinoma; when uncertain, describe findings and defer to permanent sections and thyroglobulin immunostain.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 35‑year‑old with known papillary thyroid carcinoma and enlarged lateral neck lymph node.
- IOC Request: Is this lymph node involved by metastatic papillary thyroid carcinoma?
- Description: Frozen section of lymph node shows papillary structures and clusters of cells with nuclear grooves and pseudoinclusions. Psammoma bodies may be present. The background lymphoid tissue is replaced by tumor.

## Sketch
- Papillary structures within lymphoid tissue
- Nuclear grooves and pseudoinclusions
- Psammoma bodies

## Pitfall
Benign thyroid inclusions in lymph nodes can contain follicular cells but lack papillary nuclear features; metastatic mucoepidermoid carcinoma can mimic papillary carcinoma.

## Consequence
Accurate detection of metastasis guides extent of neck dissection.

## Action
If features are classic, report metastatic papillary thyroid carcinoma; when uncertain, describe findings and defer to permanent sections and thyroglobulin immunostain.

title: Complex Sclerosing Lesion (Breast)
summary: Report as 'complex sclerosing/radial scar' if classic features are present; recommend permanent sections and immunostains to exclude tubular carcinoma.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 55‑year‑old with architectural distortion on screening mammogram.
- IOC Request: Is this radial scar/complex sclerosing lesion or invasive carcinoma?
- Description: Frozen section shows a central fibroelastotic nidus with entrapped tubules radiating outward in a stellate pattern. Tubules are lined by bland epithelium and myoepithelial cells.

## Sketch
- Central scar with radiating ducts
- Fibroelastotic stroma
- Presence of myoepithelial cell layer

## Pitfall
Frozen artifact may obscure myoepithelial cells; invasive tubular carcinoma can mimic a radial scar.

## Consequence
Mistaking a radial scar for carcinoma can prompt unnecessary resection; missing invasive carcinoma delays treatment.

## Action
Report as 'complex sclerosing/radial scar' if classic features are present; recommend permanent sections and immunostains to exclude tubular carcinoma.

title: Serous Tumor (Ovary)
summary: Classify as benign, borderline or invasive based on presence of stromal invasion; when uncertain, defer to permanents and ensure adequate sampling.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 45‑year‑old with pelvic mass; frozen section requested during staging laparotomy.
- IOC Request: Is this serous tumor benign, borderline, or invasive carcinoma?
- Description: Frozen section shows papillary structures with hierarchical branching. Borderline tumors exhibit stratified epithelium with mild nuclear atypia and no stromal invasion, whereas invasive serous carcinoma shows destructive stromal invasion by glands or solid sheets.

## Sketch
- Hierarchical papillary fronds
- Stratified epithelium in borderline tumors
- Stromal invasion in carcinoma

## Pitfall
Papillary structures in cystadenomas may mimic borderline tumors; freezing artifact can obscure subtle stromal invasion.

## Consequence
Underdiagnosing invasion may lead to inadequate staging; overdiagnosing borderline tumors as carcinoma may prompt unnecessary aggressive surgery.

## Action
Classify as benign, borderline or invasive based on presence of stromal invasion; when uncertain, defer to permanents and ensure adequate sampling.

title: Small Cell Carcinoma (Lung)
summary: If features are classic, report 'small cell carcinoma'. If uncertain, describe as 'high‑grade neuroendocrine carcinoma vs. basaloid carcinoma; recommend permanents and immunohistochemistry.'
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 70‑year‑old with rapidly growing central lung mass and paraneoplastic syndrome.
- IOC Request: Is this lesion small cell carcinoma versus non‑small cell carcinoma?
- Description: Frozen section shows sheets of small round to fusiform cells with scant cytoplasm, finely granular ('salt‑and‑pepper') chromatin and inconspicuous nucleoli. Nuclear molding and high mitotic activity are present.

## Sketch
- Sheets of small blue cells with nuclear molding
- Salt‑and‑pepper chromatin
- Crush artifact characteristic of small cell carcinoma

## Pitfall
Crush artifacts on frozen can obscure morphology; basaloid squamous carcinoma can mimic small cell carcinoma.

## Consequence
Misdiagnosis may alter adjuvant therapy (chemoradiation versus surgery).

## Action
If features are classic, report 'small cell carcinoma'. If uncertain, describe as 'high‑grade neuroendocrine carcinoma vs. basaloid carcinoma; recommend permanents and immunohistochemistry.'

title: Solid/Spindled Thyroid Lesion
summary: Describe as 'spindle/solid thyroid lesion'; comment on presence or absence of amyloid and necrosis; recommend permanent sections and immunohistochemistry for definitive typing.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 55‑year‑old with rapidly enlarging thyroid mass and compressive symptoms.
- IOC Request: Is this lesion medullary carcinoma, anaplastic carcinoma, or a benign spindle cell lesion?
- Description: Frozen section shows sheets of polygonal or spindled cells with granular cytoplasm and amyloid‑like stroma. In anaplastic carcinoma the cells are markedly pleomorphic with areas of necrosis.

## Sketch
- Sheets of spindled cells
- Amyloid‑like material in medullary carcinoma
- Marked pleomorphism in anaplastic carcinoma

## Pitfall
Spindle cell variant of papillary carcinoma and benign Hurthle cell tumors can mimic medullary carcinoma; Congo red stain on frozen sections may help identify amyloid.

## Consequence
Misclassification may alter extent of surgery and lymph node dissection.

## Action
Describe as 'spindle/solid thyroid lesion'; comment on presence or absence of amyloid and necrosis; recommend permanent sections and immunohistochemistry for definitive typing.

title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (Lung)
summary: If definitive features are present, report 'squamous cell carcinoma'. Otherwise describe as 'squamoid lesion; recommend permanent sections for classification.'
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 58‑year‑old with cough and hilar mass on imaging.
- IOC Request: Is this mass squamous cell carcinoma?
- Description: Frozen section shows nests of polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. Intercellular bridges and focal keratin pearl formation are present. Nuclear atypia is moderate to marked.

## Sketch
- Keratin pearls within tumor nests
- Intercellular bridges between squamous cells
- Peripheral palisading of tumor cells

## Pitfall
Keratinization may be focal or absent in poorly differentiated tumors; reactive squamous metaplasia can mimic carcinoma in airway lesions.

## Consequence
Overcalling metaplasia as carcinoma leads to overtreatment; undercalling poorly differentiated squamous carcinoma delays definitive surgery.

## Action
If definitive features are present, report 'squamous cell carcinoma'. Otherwise describe as 'squamoid lesion; recommend permanent sections for classification.'

title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (Head & Neck)
summary: If definitive features present, report 'squamous cell carcinoma'; otherwise describe atypia and defer to permanent sections for confirmation.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 58‑year‑old with ulcerated tonsillar mass and enlarged cervical lymph node.
- IOC Request: Is this lesion squamous cell carcinoma and are margins clear?
- Description: Frozen section shows nests of atypical squamous cells infiltrating the submucosa. Keratin pearl formation and intercellular bridges support well‑differentiated carcinoma; poorly differentiated tumors show solid sheets with necrosis.

## Sketch
- Infiltrative nests of squamous cells
- Keratin pearls and intercellular bridges
- Assess margin for tumor presence

## Pitfall
Reactive atypia in chronic tonsillitis can mimic carcinoma; frozen artifact may obscure keratinization.

## Consequence
Accurate identification of carcinoma influences extent of surgery and adjuvant therapy.

## Action
If definitive features present, report 'squamous cell carcinoma'; otherwise describe atypia and defer to permanent sections for confirmation.

title: Tubular Carcinoma (Breast)
summary: Describe features and comment on presence or absence of myoepithelial cells; recommend permanents and immunostains when uncertain.
body:
## Case Points
- Clinical Vignette: 48‑year‑old with screening‑detected 1 cm breast mass.
- IOC Request: Is this lesion invasive tubular carcinoma or sclerosing adenosis?
- Description: Frozen section shows well‑formed angulated tubules infiltrating fibrous stroma. Tubules are lined by a single layer of bland epithelial cells with open lumina. Myoepithelial cells are absent.

## Sketch
- Angulated tubules infiltrating stroma
- Single layer of epithelial cells without myoepithelium
- Open lumina in most tubules

## Pitfall
Sclerosing adenosis may mimic tubular carcinoma but retains myoepithelial cells; these may be hard to appreciate on frozen.

## Consequence
Misdiagnosis may lead to overtreatment or undertreatment and affect margin assessment.

## Action
Describe features and comment on presence or absence of myoepithelial cells; recommend permanents and immunostains when uncertain.

title: Blood Banking / Transfusion Medicine: Advanced Pathology Dossier
body:
Clinical Presentation

 Acute Transfusion Reactions
- **Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction (AHTR):** Characterized by fever, chills, hypotension, and flank pain within 24 hours (often minutes). May progress to DIC and acute renal failure.
- **TRALI (Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury):** Presents as sudden onset of hypoxemia and non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema within 6 hours of transfusion.
- **TACO (Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload):** Characterized by respiratory distress, hypertension, and elevated BNP due to volume overload.
- **Febrile Non-Hemolytic Reaction (FNHTR):** A 1°C rise in temperature; usually benign, caused by preformed cytokines in the donor unit.

 Delayed Transfusion Reactions
- **Delayed Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction (DHTR):** Occurs 3-10 days post-transfusion. Often presents with a drop in hemoglobin and mild jaundice. Classically associated with 'Kidd' (Jk) system antibodies.
- **Transfusion-Associated Graft-vs-Host Disease (TA-GVHD):** Presents with rash, diarrhea, and profound pancytopenia (90% mortality) in immunocompromised hosts.

 Macroscopic and Microscopic Hallmarks

- **Intravascular Hemolysis (AHTR):** Macroscopically, plasma and urine appear red (hemoglobinemia/hemoglobinuria). Microscopic findings include rare schistocytes and erythrophagocytosis in the spleen.
- **TRALI Pathology:** Histology reveals diffuse alveolar damage (DAD) with extensive neutrophil infiltration within the pulmonary capillaries and alveolar spaces.
- **TA-GVHD Pathology:** Microscopic evaluation shows lymphocytic infiltration of the dermis, biliary tree, and intestinal mucosa with characteristic 'satellite cell necrosis' (lymphocytes adjacent to apoptotic keratinocytes).
- **Delayed Hemolysis:** Primarily extravascular; characterized by spherocytes on peripheral smear and increased indirect bilirubin.

 High-Yield Board Pearls

- **Kleihaur-Betke Test:** Measures the amount of fetal hemoglobin in maternal circulation to calculate the required dose of RhIg (Standard 300 mcg dose covers 30 mL of fetal whole blood).
- **Irradiation:** Necessary to prevent TA-GVHD by inactivating donor T-lymphocytes; required for HLA-matched units, directed donations from relatives, or severely immunocompromised patients.
- **Leukoreduction:** Used to reduce the risk of FNHTR, CMV transmission, and HLA alloimmunization.
- **Washing:** Indicated for patients with IgA deficiency to prevent anaphylaxis from residual plasma proteins.
- **The 'Kidd' Rule:** Jk antibodies are notorious for disappearing from serum over time, leading to false-negative antibody screens and subsequent DHTR.

title: Clinical Practice Case Tutorial
body:
Clinical Vignette
A 65-year-old male with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents to the emergency department with acute onset dyspnea and chest pain radiating to his left arm. Electrocardiogram shows ST-segment elevations in leads II, III, and aVF. Initial cardiac troponin I is elevated. He is scheduled for urgent cardiac catheterization.

 Objective
*   To understand the initial diagnostic approach for acute coronary syndromes.
*   To identify key laboratory markers and their interpretation in myocardial infarction.
*   To discuss the principles of acute management strategies for STEMI.
*   To recognize the importance of timely intervention in improving patient outcomes.

 Case Discussion
The patient's presentation is highly suggestive of an acute inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). The dyspnea and chest pain are classic symptoms, and the ECG findings confirm acute ischemia with injury. Elevated cardiac troponin I, a highly specific and sensitive biomarker, indicates myocardial necrosis. The initial diagnostic workup would typically include a complete blood count, basic metabolic panel, coagulation studies, and a lipid panel, in addition to serial cardiac biomarkers and a repeat ECG. The urgent cardiac catheterization is a critical next step to identify the occluded coronary artery and facilitate revascularization, either via percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) or, in select cases, coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG). Time to revascularization is paramount in STEMI to limit infarct size and preserve myocardial function, often guided by "door-to-balloon" time metrics. Post-procedure, the patient will require antiplatelet therapy, beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, and statins for secondary prevention.

 Teaching Points
*   Acute coronary syndrome (ACS) encompasses unstable angina, NSTEMI, and STEMI, differentiated by ECG changes and cardiac biomarkers.
*   Cardiac troponins (I or T) are the preferred biomarkers for diagnosing myocardial injury due to their high specificity and sensitivity.
*   Immediate revascularization (PCI within 90 minutes for transfer patients, or thrombolysis if PCI is not available) is crucial for STEMI.
*   Secondary prevention with antiplatelets, statins, ACE inhibitors, and beta-blockers significantly reduces future cardiovascular events.
*   A comprehensive team approach involving ED physicians, cardiologists, and laboratory personnel is essential for optimal patient management.

 References
1.  Braunwald E, Zipes DP, Libby P, et al. Braunwald's Heart Disease: A Textbook of Cardiovascular Medicine. 11th ed. Elsevier; 2019.
2.  American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Task Force. 2017 AHA/ACC Clinical Practice Guidelines for STEMI. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2018;71(1):e1-e89.
3.  McPherson RA, Pincus MR. Henry's Clinical Diagnosis and Management by Laboratory Methods. 23rd ed. Elsevier; 2017.

title: Case Tutorial: Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia
body:
Case Tutorial: Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (WAIHA, Cold Agglutinin, PCH, Drug-Induced)

 Clinical_Vignette
A 58-year-old female presents with fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and scleral icterus. Her physical exam reveals pallor and mild splenomegaly. Laboratory findings include hemoglobin 6.2 g/dL, MCV 102 fL, reticulocyte count 15%, total bilirubin 3.8 mg/dL (predominantly unconjugated), and LDH 850 U/L. A peripheral blood smear shows spherocytes, polychromasia, and erythrocyte agglutination. A direct antiglobulin test (DAT) is positive for IgG and C3d.

 Objective
Upon completion of this case, the learner should be able to:
*   Classify autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) into warm, cold, paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria (PCH), and drug-induced subtypes based on clinical presentation and laboratory findings.
*   Interpret direct and indirect antiglobulin test (DAT/IAT) results in the context of AIHA diagnosis and differentiation.
*   Describe the immunopathophysiology underlying the different forms of AIHA.
*   Identify common serologic characteristics and typical antibody specificities associated with each AIHA subtype.
*   Outline the principles of laboratory investigation and initial management for a patient presenting with suspected AIHA.

 Case_Discussion
The patient's presentation with acute anemia, signs of hemolysis (elevated bilirubin, LDH, reticulocytosis), and a positive DAT strongly points to AIHA. The presence of both IgG and C3d on the DAT is classic for Warm Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (WAIHA). WAIHA is the most common form of AIHA, typically caused by IgG autoantibodies that react optimally at 37°C. These antibodies bind to red blood cell (RBC) antigens, leading to extravascular hemolysis primarily in the spleen. The spherocytes observed on the peripheral smear are a result of partial phagocytosis of antibody- or complement-coated RBCs by macrophages, losing membrane but not volume. The agglutination, while seen in many AIHAs, can be particularly pronounced in cold agglutinin disease (CAD). If the DAT were positive only for C3d, or very strongly positive for C3d with a weak or negative IgG, it would raise suspicion for Cold Agglutinin Disease (CAD) or Paroxysmal Cold Hemoglobinuria (PCH), where IgM antibodies activate complement. In CAD, high-titer IgM antibodies reacting optimally below 37°C cause agglutination and complement activation, leading to both intravascular and extravascular hemolysis. The classic specificity is anti-I. PCH, typically seen in children post-viral infection or in tertiary syphilis, involves a biphasic IgG antibody (Donath-Landsteiner antibody) that binds to RBCs in the cold and causes complement-mediated lysis upon warming. Drug-induced AIHA can manifest with various DAT patterns depending on the mechanism (e.g., drug adsorption, immune complex formation, membrane modification, autoantibody induction). Further serologic evaluation, including antibody identification and thermal amplitude studies, would confirm the specific type of AIHA and guide treatment.

 Teaching_Points
*   **DAT interpretation is key:** A positive DAT (especially IgG and C3d) is the hallmark of AIHA; specific reactivity helps differentiate subtypes.
*   **WAIHA is most common:** Characterized by IgG antibodies active at 37°C, often leading to extravascular hemolysis and spherocytosis.
*   **Cold AIHA involves IgM or biphasic IgG:** CAD involves high-titer IgM (often anti-I) causing complement activation; PCH involves a biphasic IgG (Donath-Landsteiner) primarily causing intravascular hemolysis.
*   **Peripheral smear clues:** Spherocytes suggest WAIHA, while prominent agglutination can point to cold AIHA.
*   **Drug-induced AIHA mimics other forms:** Requires careful medication history and specific serologic testing to identify the causative agent and mechanism.

 References
1.  Rodak, B. F., Carr, J. H., & Smith, L. J. (2023). _Clinical Hematology Atlas_ (6th ed.). Elsevier. 
2.  Robbins, S. L., & Cotran, R. S. (2021). _Robbins Basic Pathology_ (11th ed.). Elsevier. 
3.  Klein, H. G., & Anstee, D. J. (2014). _Mollison's Blood Transfusion in Clinical Medicine_ (12th ed.). Wiley-Blackwell.

title: Case Tutorial: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria
body:
Clinical_Vignette
A 35-year-old male presents with recurrent episodes of dark urine, particularly in the morning, severe fatigue, and abdominal pain. Initial laboratory evaluation reveals hemolytic anemia, reticulocytosis, and elevated LDH. He also reports a history of unexplained thrombotic events, including a recent portal vein thrombosis. Bone marrow examination showed features of mild hypoplasia.

 Objective
*   Understand the pathophysiology of Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH) related to the deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins.
*   Recognize the diverse clinical manifestations of PNH, including hemolytic anemia, bone marrow failure, and thrombotic complications.
*   Identify the key diagnostic method for PNH using high-sensitivity flow cytometry.
*   Discuss current therapeutic approaches for PNH, focusing on complement inhibition.

 Case_Discussion
The patient's presentation with intravascular hemolytic anemia (dark urine/hemoglobinuria, elevated LDH, reticulocytosis), severe fatigue, and particularly recurrent thrombotic events in unusual locations (portal vein thrombosis) is highly suggestive of Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH). PNH is a rare, acquired clonal hematopoietic stem cell disorder characterized by a somatic mutation in the *PIG-A* gene. This mutation leads to an inability to synthesize glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) anchors, which are crucial for attaching numerous proteins to the cell surface.

Key GPI-anchored proteins missing on PNH cells include CD55 (decay-accelerating factor) and CD59 (membrane inhibitor of reactive lysis). CD55 and CD59 normally protect cells from complement-mediated lysis by inhibiting the C3 convertase and the formation of the membrane attack complex (MAC), respectively. Without these protective proteins, red blood cells become exquisitely sensitive to complement activation, leading to chronic intravascular hemolysis, especially at night when blood pH slightly decreases, enhancing complement activity.

The thrombotic tendency, a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in PNH, is complex but likely involves complement activation on platelets and endothelial cells, leading to platelet activation and endothelial damage. The association with bone marrow hypoplasia or aplastic anemia is also well-recognized, with PNH often evolving from or coexisting with these conditions.

Diagnosis is confirmed by high-sensitivity flow cytometry, which detects the absence or marked reduction of GPI-anchored proteins (e.g., CD59 on red blood cells, CD16 and CD14 on granulocytes and monocytes) on multiple cell lineages. This test is crucial for definitive diagnosis and to quantify the size of the PNH clone. Once diagnosed, treatment with complement inhibitors, such as eculizumab or ravulizumab, is indicated. These monoclonal antibodies block the cleavage of C5, preventing MAC formation, thereby reducing hemolysis and thrombotic risk.

 Teaching_Points
*   PNH is an acquired clonal hematopoietic stem cell disorder caused by *PIG-A* gene mutation, resulting in deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins like CD55 and CD59.
*   The classic triad includes intravascular hemolytic anemia (often with dark urine), bone marrow failure, and recurrent thrombotic events (often in unusual sites).
*   Diagnosis is definitively made by high-sensitivity flow cytometry, which demonstrates the absence of GPI-anchored proteins on affected blood cell populations.
*   Complement inhibition (e.g., eculizumab, ravulizumab) is the mainstay of treatment, significantly improving patient survival and quality of life by preventing hemolysis and thrombosis.
*   Patients with aplastic anemia should be screened for PNH clones, as the two conditions are frequently associated.

 References
*   Robbins & Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease. 10th ed. Elsevier; 2021.
*   Harmening DM. Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices. 7th ed. F.A. Davis Company; 2018.
*   Rodak's Hematology: Clinical Principles and Applications. 6th ed. Elsevier; 2020.

title: Anemia in Oncology Patients: A Case Tutorial
body:
Clinical Vignette
A 62-year-old male with recently diagnosed stage IV non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC) presents for his first cycle of chemotherapy. Pre-treatment labs reveal a hemoglobin of 8.8 g/dL (baseline 12.5 g/dL three months prior), MCV 85 fL, MCH 28 pg, and RDW 15.5%. His ferritin is 350 ng/mL, serum iron 40 mcg/dL, and total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) 200 mcg/dL, with a transferrin saturation of 20%. The patient denies any gross bleeding, and his physical exam is unremarkable except for mild pallor.

 Objective
*   Identify common etiologies of anemia in oncology patients.
*   Differentiate between iron deficiency anemia and anemia of chronic disease in the context of malignancy.
*   Discuss appropriate diagnostic workup for anemia in cancer patients.
*   Outline management strategies for anemia in oncology patients, including erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) and iron supplementation.

 Case Discussion
Anemia is a common complication in oncology patients, influenced by the malignancy itself, myelosuppressive chemotherapy, radiation therapy, chronic inflammation, nutritional deficiencies, and gastrointestinal bleeding. In this patient, the normocytic, normochromic anemia (MCV 85 fL) in the setting of malignancy and elevated ferritin suggests anemia of chronic disease (ACD). While ferritin is typically low in iron deficiency anemia (IDA), it can be normal or elevated in ACD due to its role as an acute phase reactant. However, the relatively low serum iron and TIBC, along with a transferrin saturation of 20%, suggest a component of impaired iron utilization. In pure ACD, transferrin saturation is often lower (<15-20%), and TIBC is typically low. The key differentiator here is distinguishing ACD from IDA, or more commonly, a combined picture. While ferritin is elevated, it's crucial to remember that inflammation from cancer can falsely elevate ferritin, masking true iron deficiency. Further workup, such as a soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR) or bone marrow biopsy with Prussian blue stain for iron stores, could definitively distinguish between ACD and IDA, especially if iron supplementation is being considered. A sTfR/ferritin ratio >1.5 suggests functional iron deficiency even with elevated ferritin. Given the current guidelines, this patient would likely benefit from intravenous iron repletion. Chemotherapy-induced myelosuppression is another major contributor to anemia, but the anemia pre-dates chemotherapy in this vignette, indicating it's related to the cancer itself and likely an underlying iron dysregulation.

 Teaching Points
*   Anemia in oncology patients is multifactorial, including cancer-related inflammation (ACD), chemotherapy-induced myelosuppression, blood loss, and nutritional deficiencies.
*   Ferritin can be an unreliable marker for iron deficiency in cancer patients due to its elevation as an acute phase reactant; a low transferrin saturation and elevated sTfR are more indicative of functional iron deficiency.
*   The "gold standard" for assessing true iron stores remains bone marrow biopsy with Prussian blue stain, though it is rarely performed for this indication alone.
*   Management often involves addressing underlying causes, iron supplementation (oral or intravenous), and potentially erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) for chemotherapy-induced anemia in select patients, with careful consideration of risks and benefits.
*   Screening for gastrointestinal bleeding should always be considered in new or worsening anemia in oncology patients.

 References
1.  Robbins & Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease. 10th ed. Elsevier; 2021.
2.  Turgeon ML. Clinical Hematology: Theory & Procedures. 6th ed. Wolters Kluwer; 2017.
3.  Henry's Clinical Diagnosis and Management by Laboratory Methods. 24th ed. Elsevier; 2021.

title: Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) Case Tutorial
body:
Clinical Vignette
A 34-year-old female presents to the emergency department complaining of easy bruising and petechiae on her extremities for the past two weeks. She denies recent fevers, infections, medication changes, or significant past medical history. Physical examination reveals numerous petechiae, ecchymoses, and no hepatosplenomegaly. Her complete blood count shows a hemoglobin of 13.2 g/dL, white blood cell count of 7.8 x 10^9/L, and a platelet count of 8 x 10^9/L.

 Objective
*   To understand the clinical presentation and diagnostic workup for immune thrombocytopenia (ITP).
*   To differentiate primary ITP from secondary causes of thrombocytopenia.
*   To comprehend the underlying pathophysiology of platelet destruction in ITP.
*   To discuss principles of management for acute and chronic ITP.

 Case Discussion
The patient's presentation with isolated severe thrombocytopenia, mucocutaneous bleeding, and absence of other systemic symptoms or obvious causes raises strong suspicion for immune thrombocytopenia (ITP). The initial workup should exclude pseudothrombocytopenia (by reviewing a peripheral blood smear for platelet clumping), other causes of consumptive or destructive thrombocytopenia (e.g., thrombotic microangiopathies, DIC, drug-induced thrombocytopenia, sepsis), and conditions causing decreased production (e.g., bone marrow failure, nutritional deficiencies). A peripheral blood smear in ITP typically shows large platelets, which are immature megakaryocyte fragments released from the bone marrow in an attempt to compensate for peripheral destruction, and otherwise normal red and white cell morphology. Bone marrow examination is not routinely required for diagnosis in adults with typical presentations but may be considered in older patients, those not responding to initial therapy, or when other cytopenias are present to exclude myelodysplastic syndrome or other marrow disorders. The fundamental mechanism involves autoantibody production, primarily IgG, directed against platelet surface glycoproteins (e.g., GPIIb/IIIa, GPIb/IX). These antibody-coated platelets are subsequently recognized and rapidly cleared by macrophages, predominantly in the spleen, via Fc receptor-mediated phagocytosis. Impaired platelet production due to megakaryocyte damage and autoantibodies against thrombopoietin receptors also contributes to thrombocytopenia. The diagnosis of primary ITP is one of exclusion, based on isolated thrombocytopenia in the absence of other causes.

 Teaching Points
*   ITP is characterized by isolated thrombocytopenia (<100 x 10^9/L) in the absence of other identifiable causes.
*   The primary mechanism involves autoantibody-mediated platelet destruction, predominantly in the spleen, and impaired platelet production.
*   Diagnosis relies on clinical presentation, isolated thrombocytopenia, and exclusion of secondary causes (e.g., drug-induced, autoimmune diseases like SLE, viral infections like HIV/HCV).
*   Peripheral blood smear typically shows decreased platelet count with some large platelets; no schistocytes or other significant abnormalities.
*   First-line treatment for significant bleeding or very low platelet counts often includes corticosteroids or intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG).

 References
1.  Rodak, B. F., Doig, K., & Fritsma, G. A. (2023). Hematology: Clinical Principles and Applications (7th ed.). Elsevier.
2.  Shikani, R., & Staves, J. J. (2022). Technical Manual (20th ed.). AABB.
3.  Robbins, S. L., & Cotran, R. S. (2021). Robbins Basic Pathology (11th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Case Tutorial: Bleeding from Coagulation Defects
body: Clinical VignetteA 65-year-old male presents to the emergency department with new-onset epistaxis and diffuse petechiae. He has a known history of alcoholic cirrhosis and recently completed a course of broad-spectrum antibiotics for pneumonia. Laboratory results show prolonged PT, aPTT, elevated D-dimer, decreased fibrinogen, and thrombocytopenia. Peripheral smear reveals schistocytes. Objective- Understand the differential diagnosis of prolonged PT and aPTT in a bleeding patient.- Differentiate between coagulation defects caused by liver disease, vitamin K deficiency, DIC, and specific factor inhibitors.- Correlate laboratory findings with underlying pathophysiology to guide appropriate management. Case DiscussionThe patient's presentation with epistaxis, petechiae, and prolonged PT/aPTT in the context of alcoholic cirrhosis immediately raises suspicion for multiple coagulation defects. Liver disease impairs the synthesis of most coagulation factors (I, II, V, VII, IX, X, XI, XII, XIII) and inhibitors (protein C, protein S, antithrombin), leading to a complex hemostatic derangement. The prolonged PT is often more pronounced due to factor VII's short half-life, which is also vitamin K-dependent.The recent antibiotic use is a crucial clue for potential vitamin K deficiency. Broad-spectrum antibiotics can reduce gut flora that synthesize vitamin K, exacerbating pre-existing coagulopathy, especially in patients with poor nutritional status or malabsorption. Vitamin K is essential for the gamma-carboxylation of factors II, VII, IX, and X, as well as proteins C and S. Deficiency would prolong both PT and aPTT, with PT usually more sensitive. A therapeutic response to vitamin K administration would confirm this diagnosis.However, the presence of elevated D-dimer, decreased fibrinogen, thrombocytopenia, and schistocytes on peripheral smear points strongly towards disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). DIC is a pathological activation of coagulation, often triggered by severe underlying conditions like sepsis (which can complicate pneumonia in a cirrhotic patient), leading to widespread microthrombi formation and consumption of platelets and coagulation factors. The consumption coagulopathy explains the prolonged PT/aPTT and low fibrinogen, while activated fibrinolysis accounts for the elevated D-dimer. Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, evidenced by schistocytes, is a classic finding in DIC due to red cell fragmentation as they pass through fibrin strands in the microvasculature.While less likely given the full picture, isolated factor inhibitors should always be considered in unexplained prolonged PT/aPTT, especially if mixing studies show no correction. Specific inhibitors (e.g., against factor VIII) would typically prolong aPTT more significantly, and acquired inhibitors to multiple factors are rare. Differentiating these requires specialized testing beyond the initial panel. In this case, the comprehensive findings strongly favor DIC superimposed on liver disease and potential vitamin K deficiency. Teaching Points- Alcoholic cirrhosis commonly causes a complex coagulopathy due to impaired synthesis of factors, inhibitors, and often thrombocytopenia.- Vitamin K deficiency, exacerbated by antibiotics or poor nutrition, primarily affects factors II, VII, IX, X, leading to prolonged PT/aPTT.- Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) is characterized by simultaneous widespread clotting and bleeding, marked by prolonged PT/aPTT, decreased fibrinogen, thrombocytopenia, elevated D-dimer, and schistocytes.- A thorough clinical history, including medication use and underlying conditions, is critical in interpreting coagulation studies.- Consider the possibility of multiple concurrent etiologies for bleeding, especially in complex patients. References- McKenzie, S.B., & Williams, J.L. (2017). Clinical Laboratory Hematology (3rd ed.). Pearson.- McPherson, R.A., & Pincus, M.R. (Eds.). (2017). Henry's Clinical Diagnosis and Management by Laboratory Methods (23rd ed.). Elsevier.- Kumar, V., Abbas, A.K., & Aster, J.C. (2021). Robbins & Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease (10th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Granulocyte Transfusion: A Case Tutorial
body:
Granulocyte Transfusion: A Case Tutorial

 Clinical_Vignette
A 54-year-old male with acute myeloid leukemia undergoing intensive chemotherapy develops febrile neutropenia refractory to broad-spectrum antibiotics. His absolute neutrophil count (ANC) remains consistently less than 0.1 x 10^9/L for over a week, and blood cultures are positive for a multidrug-resistant Gram-negative bacillus. Despite maximal antimicrobial therapy, his clinical status continues to deteriorate with persistent fever and worsening hypotension.

 Objective
- Discuss the indications and contraindications for granulocyte transfusion.
- Describe the methods of granulocyte collection and storage.
- Explain the potential adverse reactions and complications associated with granulocyte transfusion.
- Outline the role of granulocyte transfusions in managing severe, refractory neutropenic infections.

 Case_Discussion
The patient's persistent profound neutropenia and refractory infection, particularly with a multidrug-resistant organism, make him a candidate for granulocyte transfusion. Granulocyte transfusions are typically considered for patients with severe, prolonged neutropenia (ANC < 0.5 x 10^9/L, often < 0.1 x 10^9/L) who have life-threatening bacterial or fungal infections that are unresponsive to maximal antimicrobial therapy. The mechanism of action involves providing exogenous neutrophils to temporarily bolster the patient's immune defense against the infection. Granulocytes are typically collected via apheresis from ABO-compatible donors, often stimulated with G-CSF and/or corticosteroids to increase peripheral granulocyte counts. The transfusions are usually given daily until the infection is controlled or the patient's marrow recovers. Compatibility testing (ABO and Rh matching) is crucial, and crossmatching for HLA antibodies may be considered due to the high likelihood of recipient alloimmunization. While potentially life-saving, risks include febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reactions, alloimmunization, TRALI, and viral transmission, necessitating careful risk-benefit assessment.

 Teaching_Points
- Granulocyte transfusions are a salvage therapy for severe, prolonged neutropenia with life-threatening infections refractory to antibiotics.
- Indications include profound neutropenia (ANC <0.1 x 10^9/L), documented bacterial or fungal infection, and failure to respond to appropriate antimicrobial therapy.
- Donors are often stimulated with G-CSF and/or corticosteroids; ABO/Rh compatibility is essential.
- Potential complications include febrile non-hemolytic reactions, alloimmunization, and TRALI, necessitating careful monitoring.
- Efficacy can be variable, and they are not routinely recommended outside of specific clinical scenarios or clinical trials.

 References
- Klein HG, Anstee DJ. Mollison's Blood Transfusion in Clinical Medicine. 12th ed. Wiley-Blackwell; 2014.
- Roback JD, Grossman BJ, Harris T, Hillyer CD. AABB Technical Manual. 18th ed. AABB; 2014.
- J. John, M.D. Transfusion Medicine: A Clinical Casebook. College of American Pathologists; 2017.

title: Case Tutorial: Obstetric and Pediatric Transfusion
body:
Clinical Vignette
A 3-day-old full-term male neonate presents with severe jaundice, pallor, and poor feeding. His mother is O Rh-negative, and the neonate is A Rh-positive. Initial laboratory findings show a hemoglobin of 6.0 g/dL, reticulocyte count of 15%, total bilirubin of 25 mg/dL, and a positive direct antiglobulin test (DAT). The neonate requires immediate transfusion to stabilize his condition.

 Objective
*   Understand the indications and contraindications for red blood cell and platelet transfusions in pediatric and obstetric populations.
*   Describe the special requirements for blood components (e.g., CMV-safe, irradiated, hemoglobin S negative, fresh, small aliquots) for neonates and infants.
*   Identify the unique immunological considerations, such as hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN), and the role of Rh immunoglobulin.
*   Recognize potential complications of transfusion specific to neonates, including transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD) and volume overload.

 Case Discussion
This neonate presents with severe anemia and hyperbilirubinemia, characteristic of hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDFN). The maternal O Rh-negative blood type and neonatal A Rh-positive blood type, combined with a positive DAT, strongly suggest ABO and/or Rh HDFN. The low hemoglobin (6.0 g/dL) indicates the need for red blood cell (RBC) transfusion.

When transfusing a neonate, several special considerations are paramount:
1.  **Blood Type:** For HDFN due to ABO incompatibility, Group O RBCs compatible with the mother's plasma (e.g., O Rh-negative for this case, or O Rh-positive if mother is Rh-positive) are typically used to avoid further hemolysis. If Rh HDFN is suspected, Rh-negative blood is crucial.
2.  **CMV-Safe:** All blood products for neonates and immunocompromised patients should be CMV-safe (either seronegative or leukoreduced) to prevent CMV transmission.
3.  **Irradiation:** Blood products for neonates and infants less than 4 months of age, or any intrauterine transfusions, must be irradiated to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD).
4.  **Hemoglobin S Negative:** While not always required for every neonate, for those with a risk of sickle cell disease (e.g., African ancestry, unknown family history), hemoglobin S negative units may be preferred, especially if large volume transfusions are anticipated.
5.  **Small Aliquots:** Neonates require small, weight-based aliquots of blood, often collected from a single donor unit to minimize donor exposure, reduce the risk of allergic reactions, and maintain inventory. Units are typically stored in a satellite bag or syringe for multiple small transfusions.
6.  **Freshness:** While not universally mandated, some institutions prefer "fresher" units (e.g., <7 days old) for neonates to minimize potassium load and preserve 2,3-DPG levels, although the evidence supporting this is mixed.

In this specific case, the neonate would receive Group O, Rh-negative, CMV-safe, irradiated red blood cells, likely in small aliquots, to correct anemia and support oxygen delivery, alongside phototherapy or exchange transfusion for hyperbilirubinemia.

 Teaching Points
*   Neonatal and obstetric transfusions require highly specialized blood products, including CMV-safe and irradiated components, to prevent unique complications like TA-GVHD and CMV transmission.
*   Hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN) is a critical indication for transfusion, necessitating careful selection of ABO/Rh compatible or specific blood types (e.g., O Rh-negative for ABO/Rh HDFN).
*   Rh immunoglobulin (Rhogam) is crucial in preventing Rh HDFN in Rh-negative mothers, administered antenatally and postnatally as indicated.
*   Massive transfusion protocols in obstetrics address life-threatening hemorrhage, requiring rapid, balanced component replacement.
*   Transfusion aliquots for neonates are typically small and weight-based, often from a single donor unit to limit donor exposure.

 References
*   Roback JD, et al. AABB Technical Manual. 19th ed. Bethesda, MD: AABB; 2017.
*   Klein HG, et al. Mollison's Blood Transfusion in Clinical Medicine. 12th ed. Oxford: Wiley-Blackwell; 2014.

title: Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn (HDFN) - Case Tutorial
body:
Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn (HDFN) - Case Tutorial

 Clinical Vignette
A 32-year-old G2P1 RhD-negative woman presents for routine antenatal care. Her first child required phototherapy for neonatal jaundice. Current ultrasound reveals fetal hydrops with elevated middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity (MCA-PSV), indicative of severe fetal anemia. Maternal antibody screen is positive for anti-D at a titer of 1:256, and a cordocentesis confirms severe fetal anemia requiring immediate intervention.

 Objective
*   Understand the pathophysiology of Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn (HDFN), particularly RhD alloimmunization.
*   Identify key laboratory findings and diagnostic approaches for HDFN in both maternal and fetal/neonatal samples.
*   Describe the current management strategies for HDFN, including antenatal and postnatal interventions.
*   Explain the mechanism of action and clinical utility of Rh immune globulin (RhIG) in preventing RhD alloimmunization.

 Case Discussion
This case highlights severe HDFN due to RhD alloimmunization in a multiparous RhD-negative woman. The history of neonatal jaundice in the first child, combined with a positive anti-D antibody screen, strongly suggests maternal alloimmunization from the previous pregnancy. During the current gestation, maternal IgG anti-D antibodies crossed the placenta, binding to and sensitizing the RhD-positive fetal red blood cells (RBCs). This led to extravascular hemolysis primarily in the fetal spleen and liver. The elevated MCA-PSV is a non-invasive indicator of increased blood flow in the anemic fetus, while the fetal hydrops represents a late and severe manifestation of profound anemia. Cordocentesis provides a definitive diagnosis by directly assessing fetal hemoglobin and other parameters. The primary goal of antenatal management is to prevent fetal demise by correcting severe anemia through intrauterine transfusions (IUTs). Postnatally, affected neonates may develop significant hyperbilirubinemia due to continued hemolysis of sensitized RBCs, necessitating intensive phototherapy or, in severe cases, exchange transfusion to prevent kernicterus.

 Teaching Points
*   HDFN results from maternal IgG antibodies crossing the placenta and causing immune-mediated destruction of fetal or neonatal red blood cells.
*   RhD alloimmunization is the most severe form, but antibodies to other clinically significant red cell antigens (e.g., Kell, c) can also cause HDFN.
*   Diagnosis relies on maternal antibody screening and identification, followed by fetal monitoring (e.g., MCA-PSV) and, if indicated, invasive procedures like cordocentesis.
*   Management includes antenatal intrauterine transfusions for severe fetal anemia and postnatal interventions (phototherapy, exchange transfusion) for hyperbilirubinemia.
*   Rh immune globulin (RhIG) is critical for preventing RhD alloimmunization in RhD-negative mothers at risk of exposure to RhD-positive fetal blood.

 References
1.  AABB Technical Manual. 20th ed. Bethesda, MD: AABB; 2020.
2.  Robbins Basic Pathology. 10th ed. Philadelphia, PA: Elsevier; 2018.
3.  Klein HG, Anstee DJ. Mollison's Blood Transfusion in Clinical Medicine. 12th ed. Malden, MA: Blackwell Publishing; 2014.

title: Neonatal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT) Case Tutorial
body: Clinical VignetteA term neonate presents on day of life 1 with petechiae, purpura, and bleeding from the umbilical stump. Laboratory evaluation reveals severe thrombocytopenia (platelet count 10 x 10^9/L). The mother's platelet count is normal, and she has no history of immune thrombocytopenia. Intracranial hemorrhage is suspected based on initial neurological exam. Objective*   To understand the pathophysiology of Neonatal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT).*   To identify key diagnostic features and laboratory investigations for NAIT.*   To outline the management strategies for affected neonates, including urgent interventions.*   To describe the role of maternal antibody testing and antigen typing in confirming the diagnosis and guiding future pregnancies. Case DiscussionThe clinical presentation of a full-term neonate with severe thrombocytopenia and bleeding, especially in the absence of maternal thrombocytopenia, strongly suggests an alloimmune process. NAIT occurs when a mother is sensitized to a paternal platelet-specific antigen (most commonly HPA-1a) inherited by the fetus. Maternal IgG antibodies cross the placenta, bind to fetal platelets, leading to their destruction by the fetal reticuloendothelial system, causing severe thrombocytopenia, often at birth or shortly after.Diagnosis involves demonstrating maternal antibodies against paternal platelet antigens found on fetal platelets. This typically requires testing maternal serum for anti-HPA antibodies and genotyping parental and fetal/neonatal blood for HPA antigens.In this vignette, the severe thrombocytopenia and suspected intracranial hemorrhage necessitate urgent intervention. Initial management focuses on preventing or treating hemorrhage, often with platelet transfusions. Given the alloimmune nature, random donor platelets may be destroyed. HPA-1a negative platelets (if HPA-1a is the target antigen) or maternal platelets (washed and irradiated) are the preferred choice for transfusion, as they lack the target antigen. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) may also be used to block Fc receptors and reduce platelet destruction. Teaching Points*   NAIT is the most common cause of severe isolated thrombocytopenia in otherwise healthy full-term neonates.*   The most frequently implicated antigen is HPA-1a (formerly Pl^A1), accounting for 80-90% of cases.*   Management often involves urgent transfusion of HPA-1a negative platelets or maternal platelets (washed and irradiated) to improve platelet count and stop bleeding.*   Maternal IVIG therapy during subsequent pregnancies is a common prophylactic measure for mothers with a history of NAIT.*   Differentiation from other causes of neonatal thrombocytopenia (e.g., sepsis, DIC, congenital infections) is crucial, often guided by the isolated nature of the thrombocytopenia and normal maternal platelet count. References*   Fung MK, Eder AF, Spitalnik RH, Westhoff CM, eds. Technical Manual. 19th ed. Bethesda, MD: AABB; 2017.*   Shaz BH, Hillyer CD, Winkler T. Transfusion Medicine and Hemostasis: Clinical and Laboratory Aspects. 3rd ed. Philadelphia, PA: Elsevier; 2019.*   Robbins SL, Cotran RS, Kumar V, Abbas AK, Aster JC. Robbins Basic Pathology. 10th ed. Philadelphia, PA: Elsevier; 2017.

title: Intrauterine Transfusion Case Tutorial
body:
Intrauterine Transfusion Case Tutorial

 Clinical_Vignette
A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 28 weeks gestation presents with a history of RhD alloimmunization, developing anti-D antibodies after her second pregnancy. Serial fetal ultrasounds reveal increasing ascites, skin edema, and an elevated middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity (MCA-PSV), highly suggestive of severe fetal anemia and impending hydrops fetalis. Following counseling, the decision is made to proceed with an intrauterine transfusion to prevent further complications and prolong gestation.

 Objective
*   Understand the indications and diagnostic criteria for severe fetal anemia necessitating intrauterine transfusion.
*   Describe the selection criteria and characteristics of blood products used for intrauterine transfusion.
*   Identify potential complications associated with intrauterine transfusion and post-procedure monitoring.

 Case_Discussion
Severe fetal anemia most commonly results from red blood cell alloimmunization, where maternal antibodies cross the placenta and destroy fetal red blood cells, leading to hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN). In this case, RhD alloimmunization is the etiology. Non-invasive monitoring with MCA-PSV is the primary screening tool for fetal anemia; an elevated velocity indicates increased blood flow velocity due to decreased blood viscosity, a hallmark of anemia. When MCA-PSV suggests severe anemia or hydrops is present, invasive diagnostics such as cordocentesis can confirm the diagnosis by direct fetal blood sampling and determine the degree of anemia and fetal blood type. Based on the clinical vignette, the presence of hydrops fetalis and elevated MCA-PSV strongly indicates severe fetal anemia requiring intervention.

Intrauterine transfusion (IUT) is indicated for severe fetal anemia (fetal hemoglobin less than 0.65 multiples of the median or hydrops fetalis). The procedure typically involves intravascular transfusion into the umbilical vein under ultrasound guidance. The blood product requirements are stringent: it must be O Rh-negative (universal donor type for the fetus), CMV-seronegative or leukoreduced (to prevent CMV transmission), irradiated (to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease), hemoglobin S negative (to prevent sickling in the hypoxemic fetal environment), and fresh (typically < 7 days old) to ensure optimal red cell viability and minimal potassium load. While crossmatching with maternal serum is ideal to ensure compatibility with maternal alloantibodies, O Rh-negative blood is generally considered safe if the fetal blood type is unknown or to expedite the procedure. The goal of IUT is to correct the anemia, reverse hydrops, and allow the fetus to mature further in utero.

 Teaching_Points
*   Elevated middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity (MCA-PSV) is the most reliable non-invasive method for detecting moderate to severe fetal anemia.
*   Blood products for intrauterine transfusion must be O Rh-negative, CMV-seronegative or leukoreduced, irradiated, hemoglobin S negative, and less than 7 days old.
*   Intravascular intrauterine transfusion into the umbilical vein is the preferred route for severe fetal anemia.
*   The primary indications for IUT are severe fetal anemia (Hb < 0.65 MoM) or evidence of hydrops fetalis.
*   Complications of IUT include procedure-related risks (e.g., fetal bradycardia, infection, hemorrhage), prematurity, and potential for further maternal alloimmunization.

 References
1.  Roback JD, et al. AABB Technical Manual. 20th ed. Bethesda, MD: AABB; 2020.
2.  Mithal LB, et al. Perinatal management of alloimmune hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn. UpToDate. 2023.
3.  Kleinman S, et al. Guidelines for prenatal and perinatal transfusion: AABB. Transfusion. 2011;51(6):1118-1123.

title: Case Tutorial: RhIg Calculation and Fetomaternal Hemorrhage
body:
Clinical_Vignette
A 28-year-old Rh-negative (D-negative) female delivers a healthy Rh-positive (D-positive) infant. The qualitative fetal bleed screen (rosette test) is positive. A Kleihauer-Betke (KB) test is performed, and 2000 cells are counted. 30 fetal cells are identified.

 Objective
*   Calculate the volume of fetomaternal hemorrhage (FMH).
*   Determine the appropriate number of vials of RhIg to administer.
*   Understand the principles of RhIg prophylaxis.

 Case_Discussion
RhIg is used to prevent alloimmunization (Anti-D formation) in D-negative mothers carrying D-positive fetuses. One standard vial (300 µg) of RhIg protects against a bleed of 30 mL of fetal whole blood (or 15 mL of packed RBCs). 

**Step 1: Calculate % Fetal Cells**
KB% = (Fetal Cells / Total Cells) x 100
KB% = (30 / 2000) x 100 = 1.5%

**Step 2: Calculate Volume of Bleed**
FMH Volume (mL) = KB% x 50 (constant representing maternal blood volume of 5000mL)
FMH = 1.5 x 50 = 75 mL of fetal whole blood.

**Step 3: Calculate Vials Needed**
Vials = FMH Volume / 30 mL per vial
Vials = 75 / 30 = 2.5

**Step 4: Rounding Rules**
*   If the decimal is < 0.5, round down and add 1.
*   If the decimal is ≥ 0.5, round up and add 1.
*   Here, 2.5 rounds UP to 3. Add 1 = 4 vials total.

 Teaching_Points
*   **Screening:** Rosette test is qualitative. If positive, quantify with KB or Flow Cytometry.
*   **Dosing:** 1 vial (300µg) protects against 30mL whole blood bleed.
*   **Calculation:** Always perform the rounding rule to ensure adequate coverage. Under-dosing allows immunization.
*   **Timing:** Administer within 72 hours of delivery.

 References
*   AABB Technical Manual, 20th Ed.

title: Hematopoietic Progenitor Cell (HPC) Transplantation: A Case Tutorial
body:
Clinical Vignette
A 45-year-old male with relapsed acute myeloid leukemia (AML) after induction chemotherapy is evaluated for allogeneic hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplantation. His conditioning regimen included high-dose chemotherapy and total body irradiation. He received a matched unrelated donor peripheral blood stem cell graft. On post-transplant day +15, he developed fever, generalized maculopapular rash, and watery diarrhea. Laboratory studies showed rising bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase.

 Objective
Upon completion of this case tutorial, the learner will be able to:
* Discuss the indications and contraindications for hematopoietic progenitor cell transplantation.
* Describe the essential components of pre-transplant workup for both donor and recipient, including HLA typing and infectious disease screening.
* Recognize the common complications of HPC transplantation, specifically acute graft-versus-host disease (GVHD), and interpret laboratory findings associated with its diagnosis.
* Outline the principles of GVHD prophylaxis and management.

 Case Discussion
The patient's clinical presentation with fever, rash, diarrhea, and liver dysfunction on post-transplant day +15 is highly suggestive of acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD). aGVHD is an immune-mediated complication where donor T-lymphocytes recognize host tissues as foreign and mount an inflammatory response. The target organs primarily include the skin, gastrointestinal tract, and liver, consistent with the patient's symptoms. The timing is also typical for acute GVHD, which generally occurs within the first 100 days post-transplant.

Diagnosis of aGVHD is primarily clinical, supported by laboratory findings (e.g., elevated bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase in liver involvement) and often confirmed by biopsy of affected organs (e.g., skin, GI tract). Histopathology would reveal characteristic features such as lymphocytic infiltration and epithelial cell apoptosis.

Prior to transplantation, meticulous donor selection is crucial, involving high-resolution HLA typing to minimize alloantigenic differences, thereby reducing the risk of GVHD and graft rejection. Other pre-transplant considerations include patient fitness, infectious disease screening, and the choice of conditioning regimen, which aims to eradicate recipient malignancy and create marrow space for donor cells. GVHD prophylaxis, typically involving immunosuppressive agents like cyclosporine, tacrolimus, and/or methotrexate, is initiated around the time of transplant to mitigate this risk.

 Teaching Points
* Acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) is a significant complication of allogeneic HPC transplantation, typically presenting within 100 days post-transplant with involvement of skin, GI tract, and liver.
* The pathogenesis of aGVHD involves donor T-cells reacting against recipient alloantigens.
* HLA matching is paramount in donor selection to minimize GVHD risk.
* Clinical suspicion, laboratory abnormalities (e.g., elevated liver enzymes), and histological findings from biopsy are key for aGVHD diagnosis.
* Immunosuppressive prophylaxis is standard practice to prevent aGVHD.

 References
1. Rodgers GM, et al. Rossi's Principles of Transfusion Medicine. 5th ed. Wiley-Blackwell; 2014.
2. Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease. 10th ed. Elsevier; 2020.
3. American Association of Blood Banks (AABB). Standards for Cellular Therapy Services. Latest ed. AABB Press.

title: HPC Sources (Marrow, Peripheral, Cord) Case Tutorial
body:
Clinical_Vignette
A 45-year-old male with relapsed acute myeloid leukemia is scheduled for an allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant. His matched sibling donor is unavailable, and he has identified a fully matched unrelated donor who prefers a bone marrow harvest. Concurrently, a cryopreserved umbilical cord blood unit with adequate cell dose is also identified. The transplant team must now weigh the advantages and disadvantages of bone marrow versus cord blood as hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) sources.

 Objective
